---
title: "Modules and Packages"
description: "Organize reusable code into Python files and package folders."
execute:
  echo: true
categories: [projects-and-environments]
order: 320
---
::: {.lesson-meta}
**Level:** Beginner to intermediate
**Estimated time:** 25–40 minutes
**You will learn:** Organize reusable code into Python files and package folders.
**Practice in:** VS Code and the terminal
:::

## Why this matters

Organize reusable code into Python files and package folders.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one clear explanation.

::: {.callout-note title="Guiding questions"}
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Modules and Packages** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Learning objectives"}
You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 2 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- **1. Modules** — Put reusable Python code in .py files and import it elsewhere.
- **2. Packages** — Group related modules into folders with importable names.

## 1. Modules

Put reusable Python code in .py files and import it elsewhere.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
A module is like a toolbox drawer: open it when you need a tool stored there.
:::

### What this means

A module is a Python file that can define functions, classes, and constants for reuse.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import math
print(math.sqrt(16))
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `import math` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `print(math.sqrt(16))` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
Naming your file the same as a standard library module, such as math.py, can shadow the
real module.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "projects-and-environments-modules",
  "title": "Modules checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Modules?",
      "options": [
        "A module is a Python file that can define functions, classes, and constants for reuse.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A module is a Python file that can define functions, classes, and constants for reuse."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A module is like a toolbox drawer: open it when you need a tool stored there."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 2. Packages

Group related modules into folders with importable names.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
A package is a labeled binder with several sections, not a pile of loose papers.
:::

### What this means

A package is a folder of modules that belong together.

### First example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
from pathlib import Path
print(Path("src") / "my_project")
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `from pathlib import Path` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.
2. `print(Path("src") / "my_project")` — identify the value, name, or action on this line.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the first example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
Imports depend on project layout. Run code from the project root while learning to
reduce path surprises.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "projects-and-environments-packages",
  "title": "Packages checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Packages?",
      "options": [
        "A package is a folder of modules that belong together.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A package is a folder of modules that belong together."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A package is a labeled binder with several sections, not a pile of loose papers."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the first example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
first example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.callout-tip title="Key points"}
- **Modules**: A module is a Python file that can define functions, classes, and constants for reuse.
- **Packages**: A package is a folder of modules that belong together.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python modules tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html
- venv documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html
- argparse documentation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/argparse.html
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
